rulings.Case Title: SIERRA POINT LUMBER AND PLYWOOD COMPANY, INC., A C VS. DONG XING FU et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Sanction Demas Yan, Tina Yan, And Mark Lapham And To Set Aside And Void (1)Judgment Of January 14, 2015 And (2) Acknowledgment Of Assignment Of Judgment Of August 5, 2019 And To Deem Or Declare Demas Yan And Tina Yan To Be Alter Ego Of Legal Recovery, Llc
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, December 12, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT DONG FU's Motion To Sanction Demas Yan, Tina Yan, And Mark Lapham And To Set Aside And Void (1)Judgment Of January 14, 2015 And (2) Acknowledgment Of Assignment Of Judgment Of August 5, 2019 And To Deem Or Declare Demas Yan And Tina Yan To Be Alter Ego Of Legal Recovery, LLC. Defendant Dong Xing Fu's motion to sanction Demas Yan, Tina Yan and Mark Lapham, to set aside the judgment of January 14, 2015 and the assignment of that judgment, and to declare Demas Yan and Tina Yan to be the alter ego of Legal Recovery, LLC is granted in part. No opposition filed. Absent an order of the Bankruptcy Court or this court, neither Golden State Lumber, Inc. nor Legal Recovery, LLC may take any steps to enforce the judgment of January 14, 2015 entered in this case. Mr. Fu has shown that the judgment has been discharged by the Bankruptcy Court. Because Demas Yan and Tina Yan are not parties to this case, this court lacks jurisdiction to issue any orders against them in this case. While Mr. Lapham filed a notice of assignment of the judgment, there has been insufficient showing that his doing warrants sanctions by this court. There is no need to set aside the judgment or the assignment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Mr. Fu is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: SANDRA FUNES VS. SAN FRANCISCO CITY AND COUNTY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing And Motion For Peremptory Writ Of Administrative Mandamus
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, December 12, 2019, Line 2, PETITIONER SANDRA FUNES' Motion For Peremptory Writ Of Administrative Mandamus. Petitioner Funes petition for writ of administrative mandamus is denied. Substantial evidence supports respondent's conclusion that petitioner's EEO complaints were untimely. "Substantial evidence" is defined as evidence of "ponderable legal significance…reasonable in nature, credible, and of solid value…[and] relevant evidence that a reasonable mind might accept as adequate to support a conclusion." (Young v. Gannon (2002) 97 Cal.App.4th 209, 225.) The parties agree that "[u]nder the City's internal complaint procedures, complaints of discrimination must be filed within 180 calendar days of the date the allege discriminatory action took place or the date the employee would have first become aware of the discriminatory action." (AR 77.) Petitioner contends that her first EEO complaint filed on 1/31/17 was timely because she learned of discriminatory acts regarding her co-workers in 11/16. The record, however, indicates that petitioner could have filed her EEO complaint within 180 days of her reinstatement denial, which occurred on 4/13/16. Petitioner filed a DFEH complaint regarding the same incidents on 9/20/16 and nothing prevented her from seeking relief before respondent during the 180-day period. (AR 68-70; 74.) Petitioner fails to show that respondent's failure to waive the limitations period was arbitrary or an abuse of discretion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for respondent is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: KRISTINA NENAYDOKH VS. GREG SHENKMAN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Re: Hearing For Contempt (Per Order Filed 10-30-19)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, December 12, 2019, Line 1, Order To Show Cause Re: Hearing For Contempt (Per Order Filed 10-30-19) Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Appearance Required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANGES PAK VS. GITHUB, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Portions Of Plaintiff Opposition To Motion For Summary Judgment And Or Summary Adjudication And Supporting Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, December 12, 2019, Line 5, 1 - DEFENDANT GITHUB, INC.'s Motion To Seal Portions Of Plaintiff's Opposition To Motion For Summary Judgment And Or Summary Adjudication And Supporting Documents. Defendant Github, Inc.'s motion to seal portions of plaintiff's opposition to motion for summary judgment and/or summary adjudication and supporting documents is granted. No opposition filed and good cause shown. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The proposed orders submitted by Github contemplate that the court will seal portions of a document. That is not the current procedure. The current procedure is the court seals the entirety of a document and, when there are portions of a document that are not covered by a sealing order, the party filing the document is required to submit a redacted version of the document redacting only the portions that are covered by a sealing order. Counsel for Github is required to modify its proposed orders to reflect the current procedure. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: HERCULES MOSS VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tranfer Venue From The County Of San Francisco Superior Court To Sacramento County Superior Court; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Chase R. Morello
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, December 12, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT JULIE MESMAN's Motion To Tranfer Venue From The County Of San Francisco Superior Court To Sacramento County Superior Court. Defendant Julie Anne Mesman's motion to change venue is denied. Defendant's motion to change venue under Code Civ. Proc. § 397(a)'s mandatory grounds is denied. On a motion to change venue, the burden is on the moving party to show (1) that venue is proper in a different county than the one in which the lawsuit was filed and (2) that venue is improper in the current county under any applicable theory. (La Mirada Community Hospital v. Superior Court (1967) 249 Cal.App.2d 39, 42.) Although Defendant has established that Sacramento County would be a proper venue, it has failed to show that venue is improper in San Francisco County under any applicable theory. Under Code of Civ. Proc. § 395(a) an action for personal injury is proper "in either the county where the injury occurs or the injury causing death occurs or the county where the defendants, or some of them reside." Although Defendant Mesman does not reside in San Francisco County, Defendant Uber Technologies' principal place of business is. (Kang Decl. ISO Opp., Ex. C.) Thus, venue is proper in San Francisco. (Code Civ. Proc. § 395.5 ["A corporation may be sued in the county . . . the principal place of business of such corporation is situated"].) Defendant's motion to change venue under Code Civ. Proc. § 397(c)' discretionary grounds is denied. Under a § 397(c) motion, "only convenience to third-party disinterested witnesses" may be considered. (Corfee, 202 Cal.App.2d at 478.) The convenience of the parties may not be considered. Thus, defendant's primary reliance on the purported inconvenience of the San Francisco forum to itself is misplaced. Defendant does not sufficiently identify or establish inconvenience of any other witness. Such a showing must be done "through affidavits which contain something more than generalities and conclusions." (Corfee, 202 Cal.App.2d at 477.) "[T]he supporting declarations must show (1) the name of each witness, (2) the expected testimony of each witness, and (3) facts showing why the attendance of said witnesses at trial will be inconvenient at trial and why the ends of justice would be served by a transfer." (Stute v. Burinda (1981) 123 Cal.App.Supp.3d 11, 17.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: SARAS WATI ET AL VS. CLASSIC SHUTTLE SERVICES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, December 12, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANTS CLASSIC SHUTTLE SERVICES, INC., XIONGYING CHEN's MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE. Defendants' motion to change venue to San Mateo County is denied. CCP § 397(c) permits transfer from one proper county to another proper county when "the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the change." The burden is on the party seeking a change of venue to prove 1) that the convenience of witnesses and 2) that the ends of justice will be promoted by a change. (Corfee v. Southern California Edison Co. (1962) 202 Cal.App.2d 473, 477.) To show the convenience of witnesses, the moving party "must do through affidavits which contain something more than generalities and conclusions. Affidavits or declarations in support of the motion must set forth the names of the witnesses, the nature of the testimony expected from each, and the reasons why the attendance of each would be inconvenient." (Corfee, 202 Cal.App.2d, at 477.) Defendants argue that the convenience of witnesses here is that "the residence of the witnesses is in the county to which the transfer of the cause of action is requested." (Defs.' Mot. 6:22-23.) However, the "convenience of the witnesses in attending the trial is the controlling element in connection with such a motion, rather than the place of their actual residence." (Dick v. La Madrid (1947) 82 Cal.App.2d 450, 452.) It does not matter that witnesses live in a different county if the moving party does not show that a change would actually be more convenient. (See Id. [holding in a motion to change venue from Kern to LA County that alleging that testifying Los Angeles police officers were Los Angeles officers was insufficient without an actual showing of inconvenience because it is "possible, however improbable it may be, that they would welcome an opportunity to leave Los Angeles for a short time."].) Defendants fail to show any actual inconvenience, beyond claims that the witnesses live or work in San Mateo County. Mr. Misa testifies that it "would be more convenient . . . to testify in Redwood City" not that it would be inconvenient to testify in San Francisco. (Cholakian Decl., Ex. E, Misa Depo. 36:12-15; see Corfee, 202 Cal.App.2d at 477 [holding that affidavits must show why the attendance of witnesses "would be inconvenient."].) Officer Chinca testifies that the difference between San Francisco and Redwood City is "kind of a toss-up." (Cholakian Decl., Ex. F, Chinca Depo. 35:17.) Officer Smithmatungol testified that location does not "really matter . . . Either/or works for me." Rose Decl., Ex. C, Smithmatungol Depo. 22:14-17.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: COLBY VELASQUEZ VS. COSCO FIRE PROTECTION INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, December 12, 2019, Line 7, 1 - DEFENDANT RIDGE TOOL COMPANY's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Ridge Tool Company's demurrer to the fourth cause of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Coby Velasquez is off calendar and Mr. Velasquez is granted leave to file his proposed second amended complaint after deleting all references to punitive damages against Ridge Tool. The court construes Mr. Velasquez's untimely opposition as a request for leave to file his proposed second amended complaint and grants such leave in accordance with California's strong policy favoring allowance of amendments to pleadings. The court discerns no prejudice to Ridge Tool in allowing the filing of a second amended complaint. Ridge Tool's argument in its reply memorandum that the fourth cause of action in the second amended complaint is subject to a demurrer lacks merit. The fourth cause of action in the second amended complaint adequately alleges a valid cause of action for products liability against Ridge Tool notwithstanding that some of the facts supporting the fourth cause of action are arguably inconsistent with facts alleged in the first three causes of action against other defendants. It is well-settled that a plaintiff may allege inconsistent facts in an unverified complaint in different causes of action. (See. e.g., Rutter Group Guide on Civil Procedure Before Trial at 6:242-244). The insufficiency of the punitive damages allegations against Ridge Tool is addressed in the order granting Ridge Tool's motion to strike the punitive damages allegations against it. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Velasquez is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: COLBY VELASQUEZ VS. COSCO FIRE PROTECTION INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From The First Amended Complaint; Declaration Of Paul T. Martin
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, December 12, 2019, Line 7, 2 - DEFENDANT RIDGE TOOL COMPANY's Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From The First Amended Complaint. Defendant Ridge Tool Company's motion to strike the punitive damages allegations alleged against it in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Coby Velasquez is granted without prejudice to Mr. Velasquez's right to file a motion seeking leave to amend to allege punitive damages allegations against Ridge Tool if and when Mr. Velasquez discovers or otherwise learns of and is able to plead facts supporting punitive damages liability against Ridge Tool. No opposition filed and good cause shown. Mr. Velasquez has not alleged sufficient facts showing despicable conduct by Ridge Tool nor does it appear that at this stage of the case he is able to do so. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Renew A Judgment Nunc Pro Tunc As Of 6/3/19 That Was Rejected On 5/29/19

Plaintiff Brian McGill's motion to renew judgment nunc pro tunc to June 3, 2019 is granted. No opposition filed and good cause shown. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the court will sign the proposed order submitted by Mr. McGill. =(302/HEK)
